county_metadata.parent_county: Danish West Indies colony (acquired from France, 1733)
county_metadata.record_losses: Some colonial-era records were damaged by hurricanes, fires, and the 1878 labor revolt (Fireburn) on St. Croix. Many Danish colonial administrative records were transferred to the Danish National Archives in Copenhagen when the US purchased the islands in 1917. Local court and property records from the Danish period survive in fragmentary form at the National Archives in Washington (NARA) and at local government offices.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: St. Croix (Danish: Sankt Croix) is the largest island in the USVI at approximately 84 square miles. Acquired by Denmark from France in 1733, St. Croix became the center of the Danish sugar plantation economy. The island has two main towns: Christiansted (the former Danish colonial capital) and Frederiksted (on the western coast, site of the 1848 emancipation proclamation and the 1878 Fireburn labor revolt). At its peak, St. Croix had over 300 sugar plantations worked by enslaved Africans. Danish Governor Peter von Scholten proclaimed emancipation on July 3, 1848, freeing approximately 5,000 enslaved persons. The island was purchased by the United States in 1917 for $25 million along with St. Thomas and St. John. The district seat is Christiansted.
county_metadata.research_tips: St. Croix is the largest of the USVI and was the center of the Danish sugar plantation economy. The Florence Williams Public Library in Christiansted holds local history collections including plantation records, maps, and photographs. For Danish colonial records (1733-1917), the Danish National Archives (Rigsarkivet) in Copenhagen holds the Vestindisk-guineisk Rente- samt Bogholderi-Kontor (West Indian and Guinean Accounting Office) records, governor's reports, census returns, land registers (matrikler), and church registers (kirkeboger) -- many are digitized at the Danish Archival Information System (Daisy/Arkivalieronline). The 1846 and earlier Danish census records enumerate enslaved persons by name on St. Croix plantations. The Moravian Church (Herrnhuter Brudergemeine) maintained meticulous baptism, marriage, and burial records for both enslaved and free congregants from the 1730s -- originals at the Moravian Archives in Bethlehem, PA, and Herrnhut, Germany. Lutheran (Danish State Church) records from the 1740s and Catholic parish records are also valuable. After emancipation in 1848, labor contracts (kontraktboger) document formerly enslaved workers and their families. NARA holds US-period USVI records from 1917 forward, including Record Group 55 (Government of the Virgin Islands). The St. Croix Landmarks Society preserves plantation ruins and associated historical records. FamilySearch has some microfilmed USVI church and civil records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Danish matrikler (cadastral registers) from the 1750s partially digitized; US-period land records from 1917 at USVI Recorder of Deeds in Christiansted

source_profiles.source_name: St. Croix Danish Colonial Census Records -- Danish National Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Danish census returns for St. Croix from 1841 enumerate free and enslaved persons by plantation and town; earlier matrikler (land registers) from the 1750s list slaveholders and enslaved persons by number; post-1917 US census records at NARA and FamilySearch
source_profiles.survival_notes: Fragmentary survival for pre-1841 enumerations; 1841-1911 Danish census records are substantially complete and many are digitized at the Danish Archival Information System (Arkivalieronline)

source_profiles.source_name: St. Croix Church Records -- Moravian, Lutheran, Catholic
source_profiles.repository: Moravian Archives, Bethlehem PA / Danish National Archives / Local parishes
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Moravian Church (Friedensthal, Friedensberg, Friedensfeld missions) baptism, marriage, and burial records from the 1730s for enslaved and free congregants; Lutheran (Danish State Church) records from the 1740s; Catholic parish records (Holy Cross, Christiansted) from the 1770s; these are the primary genealogical sources for colonial-era St. Croix families
source_profiles.survival_notes: Moravian records are well-preserved at the Moravian Archives in Bethlehem, PA, and Herrnhut, Germany; Lutheran parish registers at the Danish National Archives are substantially complete; some Catholic records have gaps due to hurricane damage

source_profiles.source_name: St. Croix Enslaved Persons, Plantation, and Emancipation Records
source_profiles.repository: Danish National Archives, Copenhagen / NARA / Florence Williams Public Library, Christiansted
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Plantation inventories listing enslaved persons by name, age, and occupation; slave trade records; matrikler (land and slave registers) from the 1750s; emancipation records from 1848; post-emancipation labor contracts (kontraktboger) documenting formerly enslaved workers and their families through the 1878 Fireburn period; estate inventories and probate records naming enslaved persons
source_profiles.survival_notes: Danish colonial slave records are surprisingly detailed compared to many Caribbean colonies; the matrikler system provides systematic enumeration from the mid-1700s; post-emancipation labor contracts are substantially complete

source_profiles.source_name: St. Croix Land Records -- Danish Matrikler and US-Period Deeds
source_profiles.repository: Danish National Archives, Copenhagen / USVI Recorder of Deeds / NARA
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Danish matrikler (cadastral registers) from the 1750s document plantation boundaries, owners, and enslaved labor forces; Danish-period deeds and mortgages; US-period land records from 1917 at the USVI Recorder of Deeds in Christiansted; NARA holds some transferred Danish land records
source_profiles.survival_notes: Matrikler are substantially complete from the mid-1700s; Danish-period deed books have some gaps; US-period records are complete from 1917

source_profiles.source_name: St. Croix Probate and Estate Records -- Danish and US Period
source_profiles.repository: Danish National Archives, Copenhagen / USVI Superior Court / NARA
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Danish-period probate records (skifteprotokoller) from the Christiansted and Frederiksted courts document estates of free persons including inventories of enslaved persons and property; US-period probate from 1917 at the USVI Superior Court; many Danish probate records are at the Danish National Archives and partially digitized
source_profiles.survival_notes: Danish probate records are valuable because they enumerate all property including enslaved persons by name; US-period records are complete

source_profiles.source_name: St. Croix Birth, Death, and Marriage Records -- US Period
source_profiles.repository: USVI Department of Health, Christiansted / NARA
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Birth, death, and marriage certificates from the US period (1917-present) at the USVI Department of Health; earlier vital events are documented in church records and Danish civil registers; some Danish civil registration records from the 1860s survive at the Danish National Archives

source_profiles.source_name: St. Croix Historic Newspapers -- Danish and US Period
source_profiles.repository: Enid M. Baa Public Library / Florence Williams Public Library / Library of Congress
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Danish-language and English-language newspapers from St. Croix including the Royal Danish American Gazette (1770-1802), St. Croix Avis (1844-1917), and US-period papers; contain legal notices, estate advertisements, runaway slave notices, vital event announcements, and shipping news

county_metadata.record_losses: St. John's small population and the 1733 slave revolt resulted in limited colonial record survival. Most plantation records were maintained by individual estates; some survive at the Danish National Archives in Copenhagen. The establishment of Virgin Islands National Park in 1956 preserved archaeological and plantation sites but did not recover paper records. Local government records are held at the St. John Municipal Building in Cruz Bay.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: St. John (Danish: Sankt Jan) is the smallest USVI district by population (approximately 4,000 residents) and the third-largest by area at about 20 square miles. Colonized by Denmark in 1718, the island was developed as a sugar and cotton plantation economy. The 1733 slave revolt -- one of the first and longest in the Americas -- saw enslaved Akwamu people seize control of the island for approximately six months before being suppressed with French military assistance from Martinique. By the 19th century, the plantation economy had declined, and much of the island reverted to bush. In 1956, Laurance Rockefeller donated approximately 5,000 acres to establish Virgin Islands National Park, which now covers about two-thirds of the island. The district seat is Cruz Bay.
county_metadata.research_tips: St. John is the smallest USVI district by population and is dominated by Virgin Islands National Park, which covers approximately two-thirds of the island. Genealogical research for St. John families is challenging due to the island's small population, limited institutional record-keeping, and the devastation of the 1733 slave revolt. The National Park Service archives at St. John contain archaeological surveys, plantation site documentation, and oral history recordings relevant to families with connections to the island's 109+ historic sugar plantations. The Danish National Archives in Copenhagen holds the most complete colonial records for St. John, including land registers, governor's correspondence, and census records from the 18th and 19th centuries. Moravian Church records are particularly important for St. John -- the Bethany Moravian Church served enslaved and free persons from the 1750s, and records are at the Moravian Archives in Bethlehem, PA. The Emmaus Moravian Church records also contain vital records for St. John families. For the US period (1917-present), records are at NARA (Record Group 55) and local government offices in Cruz Bay. The St. John Historical Society maintains local history collections and oral histories. Due to the island's small size, many St. John families have connections to St. Thomas or the British Virgin Islands -- cross-referencing records from those jurisdictions is essential.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: NPS historical documentation of plantation sites; Danish matrikler at Danish National Archives; US-period land records from 1917 at USVI Recorder of Deeds

source_profiles.source_name: St. John Church Records -- Moravian and Lutheran
source_profiles.repository: Moravian Archives, Bethlehem PA / Danish National Archives, Copenhagen
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Moravian Church records from Bethany and Emmaus congregations on St. John from the 1750s; these are the most complete genealogical source for St. John families, recording baptisms, marriages, and burials for both enslaved and free persons; Lutheran (Danish State Church) records from the 1740s are at the Danish National Archives
source_profiles.survival_notes: Moravian records are well-preserved at the Moravian Archives in Bethlehem, PA; Lutheran records have some gaps; the 1733 slave revolt disrupted early record-keeping

source_profiles.source_name: St. John Danish Colonial Census and Plantation Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Danish census returns for St. John from 1841; earlier plantation inventories and matrikler from the 1700s; St. John's small population means census records are compact but detailed; post-1917 US census records at NARA and FamilySearch
source_profiles.survival_notes: Pre-1841 records are fragmentary; Danish census records from 1841-1911 are substantially complete

source_profiles.source_name: St. John Enslaved Persons and Plantation Records
source_profiles.repository: Danish National Archives, Copenhagen / National Park Service / NARA
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Plantation inventories from St. John's 109+ sugar estates listing enslaved persons; records of the 1733 slave revolt (one of the earliest and longest in the Americas); matrikler enumerating enslaved persons by plantation; emancipation records from 1848; NPS archaeological surveys of plantation sites provide context for enslaved communities
source_profiles.survival_notes: Early records are sparse due to the 1733 revolt and the island's small administrative apparatus; Danish National Archives holds the most complete colonial records; NPS holds archaeological documentation

source_profiles.source_name: St. John Land Records -- Danish Matrikler and NPS Plantation Documentation
source_profiles.repository: Danish National Archives, Copenhagen / National Park Service / USVI Recorder of Deeds
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Danish matrikler (cadastral registers) documenting plantation boundaries and ownership on St. John; NPS historical documentation of plantation sites within Virgin Islands National Park including Annaberg, Cinnamon Bay, and Reef Bay plantations; US-period land records from 1917 at the USVI Recorder of Deeds
source_profiles.survival_notes: Danish land records for St. John are less complete than for St. Croix; NPS documentation is thorough for properties within park boundaries

source_profiles.source_name: St. John Probate and Estate Records -- Danish and US Period
source_profiles.repository: Danish National Archives, Copenhagen / USVI Superior Court / NARA
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Danish-period probate records (skifteprotokoller) for St. John estates; these document property transfers including enslaved persons; US-period probate from 1917 at the USVI Superior Court; the small number of estates on St. John means probate records are limited but detailed
source_profiles.survival_notes: Fragmentary for the early colonial period; more complete from the late 1700s forward

county_metadata.record_losses: Charlotte Amalie experienced fires in 1804, 1806, and 1826 that destroyed portions of the town and some government records. Hurricane damage has also affected records over the centuries. Danish colonial administrative records were largely transferred to the Danish National Archives in Copenhagen in 1917. The Enid M. Baa Public Library and VI government offices hold surviving local records.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: St. Thomas (Danish: Sankt Thomas) is the second-largest USVI island at approximately 32 square miles and home to the territorial capital, Charlotte Amalie. Colonized by Denmark in 1666, St. Thomas was primarily a commercial and trading center rather than an agricultural island. Charlotte Amalie's deep natural harbor made it one of the busiest ports in the Caribbean during the 18th and 19th centuries. The island attracted a diverse population including Danish administrators, Sephardic Jewish merchants, free people of color, enslaved Africans, and immigrants from across Europe and the Caribbean. The transfer to the United States occurred on March 31, 1917. Charlotte Amalie serves as both the territorial capital and the district seat.
county_metadata.research_tips: St. Thomas was the commercial and administrative capital of the Danish West Indies, and Charlotte Amalie was one of the busiest ports in the Caribbean. The Enid M. Baa Public Library in Charlotte Amalie holds the most important local history collection in the USVI, including manuscripts, photographs, newspapers (including The Daily Herald and its predecessors), maps, and genealogical files. The Danish National Archives (Rigsarkivet) in Copenhagen holds the bulk of colonial administrative records for St. Thomas, including the governor's office records, court records (Vestindisk Byfoged), customs records, census returns, and vital records registers -- many are accessible through the Danish Archival Information System (Arkivalieronline). Church records are critical: the Frederick Lutheran Church (Danish State Church, from 1666) and the Dutch Reformed Church (from 1660) have the earliest records; Moravian Church records from the 1730s are at the Moravian Archives in Bethlehem, PA; All Saints Cathedral (Anglican/Episcopal, from 1848) and Sts. Peter and Paul Cathedral (Catholic, from 1802) serve later periods. The Sephardic Jewish community (Beracha Veshalom Vegimulth Hasidim congregation, est. 1796) maintained birth, marriage, and burial records -- Charlotte Amalie had one of the oldest synagogues in continuous use in the Western Hemisphere. NARA holds US-period records (Record Group 55). For immigration research, St. Thomas was a free port that attracted immigrants from across the Caribbean, Europe, and the Middle East. FamilySearch has microfilmed some USVI vital and church records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Danish-period property records partially accessible through Danish National Archives; US-period land records from 1917 at USVI Recorder of Deeds in Charlotte Amalie

source_profiles.source_name: St. Thomas Church Records -- Lutheran, Moravian, Dutch Reformed, Catholic, Jewish
source_profiles.repository: Danish National Archives / Moravian Archives, Bethlehem PA / Local congregations / American Jewish Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Frederick Lutheran Church (Danish State Church, from 1666) baptism, marriage, and burial records; Dutch Reformed Church records from the 1660s; Moravian Church records from the 1730s at the Moravian Archives in Bethlehem, PA; All Saints Cathedral (Anglican/Episcopal, from 1848); Sts. Peter and Paul Cathedral (Catholic, from 1802); Beracha Veshalom Vegimulth Hasidim synagogue records (Sephardic Jewish, est. 1796) -- Charlotte Amalie's synagogue is one of the oldest in continuous use in the Western Hemisphere
source_profiles.survival_notes: Lutheran and Dutch Reformed records are the earliest and most complete; Moravian records are well-preserved; synagogue records are valuable for the Sephardic merchant community; some records were damaged in the Charlotte Amalie fires of 1804, 1806, and 1826

source_profiles.source_name: St. Thomas Danish Colonial Census Records -- Danish National Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Danish census returns for St. Thomas and Charlotte Amalie from 1841; earlier population counts and matrikler from the 1700s; Charlotte Amalie's status as a major port means census records capture a diverse international population; post-1917 US census records at NARA and FamilySearch
source_profiles.survival_notes: Danish census records from 1841-1911 are substantially complete; earlier enumerations are fragmentary; the diverse and transient port population means some residents may not appear in census records

source_profiles.source_name: St. Thomas Enslaved Persons and Free Persons of Color Records
source_profiles.repository: Danish National Archives, Copenhagen / Enid M. Baa Public Library / NARA
source_profiles.coverage_notes: St. Thomas had a large free persons of color community in addition to enslaved persons. Records include: manumission documents, free-colored registers, slave sale and purchase records, plantation inventories, matrikler, and emancipation records from 1848. Charlotte Amalie's role as a slave trading port means records also document the transatlantic trade. The Danish National Archives holds the most systematic records.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Records are more urban-focused than the plantation records of St. Croix; free persons of color documentation is relatively strong; slave trade port records provide additional documentation

source_profiles.source_name: St. Thomas Immigration and Port Records -- Danish and US Period
source_profiles.repository: Danish National Archives, Copenhagen / NARA / Enid M. Baa Public Library
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Charlotte Amalie was a major free port attracting immigrants from across the Caribbean, Europe, and the Middle East. Danish customs and port records document arriving passengers and merchants; naturalization records from both Danish and US periods; US-period immigration records from 1917 at NARA (Record Group 85). The diverse immigrant population included Sephardic Jews, French Huguenots, Scottish and English merchants, and laborers from other Caribbean islands.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Danish port records survive in fragmentary form at the Danish National Archives; US-period immigration records are substantially complete from 1917

source_profiles.source_name: St. Thomas Land Records -- Danish Period and US Deeds
source_profiles.repository: Danish National Archives, Copenhagen / USVI Recorder of Deeds / NARA
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Danish-period property records for St. Thomas including town lot records for Charlotte Amalie, rural plantation matrikler, and deeds and mortgages; US-period land records from 1917 at the USVI Recorder of Deeds; Charlotte Amalie's dense urban property records are detailed compared to rural plantation records
source_profiles.survival_notes: Some Charlotte Amalie property records were damaged in the fires of 1804, 1806, and 1826; Danish National Archives holds the most complete colonial land records; US-period records are complete from 1917

source_profiles.source_name: St. Thomas Birth, Death, and Marriage Records -- US Period
source_profiles.repository: USVI Department of Health, Charlotte Amalie / NARA
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Birth, death, and marriage certificates from the US period (1917-present) at the USVI Department of Health; earlier vital events documented in church records and some Danish civil registers from the 1860s at the Danish National Archives

source_profiles.source_name: St. Thomas Historic Newspapers -- Danish and US Period
source_profiles.repository: Enid M. Baa Public Library / Library of Congress / Danish National Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Charlotte Amalie newspapers including the St. Thomas Tidende (Danish-language, from 1770), The Daily Herald (English-language), and other publications; contain legal notices, shipping news, estate advertisements, vital event announcements, and slave sale notices; the Enid M. Baa Public Library holds the most complete local newspaper collection
